coredata.dc:title: Thermal modeling of a subterranean battery energy storage system for residential and commercial buildings
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Oyekola, Elizabeth
Swan, Lukas
Dahn, J.R.
coredata.dc:description: Battery systems are now routinely deployed at residential and commercial buildings to manage solar photovoltaic generation and electricity demand peaks. The siting of the battery is an important consideration for safety, thermal management, and size (footprint; physical presence). Subterranean (underground) installations have yet to be considered in detail, although they offer benefits in terms of these three considerations. We develop a subterranean battery finite element analysis model to investigate the thermal behaviour and energy performance. The model is calibrated and verified by comparison to experimental testing. The model is then used to investigate performance of the battery while subject to a range of use case application signals, soil thermal conditions, geographic region (soil temperature), and battery geometry. The model can be used to identify the impact of climatic conditions, shape choices, and restrictions on cycling operations due to temperature limitations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion
Battery
Subterranean
Thermal modeling, grid storage
Solar

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was made possible with funding from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, # NETGP 468468-14 ) through support of the Strategic Network of Energy Storage Technology (NESTnet) based at Toronto Metropolitan University (formerly Ryerson University). Additional support was provided by the Vanier-Banting Secretariat , the Province of Nova Scotia through Graduate Scholarships, and the Faculty of Engineering, Dalhousie University . The support and provision from the NOVONIX group, Canada and the Renewable Energy Storage Laboratory, Dalhousie University is appreciated. We also appreciate residential and commercial building data obtained from EMERA Energy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram showing full hexagonal pack and assumption of the model used.
Diagram showing the thermal response across all sides of the hexagonal pack (black lines are the simplistic hexahedral mesh used for this example).
Model parts and geometric structure (in meters).
Geometric model showing adiabatic faces and isothermal face.
Current, voltage, heat generation rate and electrical power profiles of the LF signal.
Current, voltage, heat generation rate, and electrical power profiles of the PV signal.
Diagram showing the location of temperature measured in the soil.
Modeled temperatures (M) and experimental result temperatures (T) in the battery module and soil while subject to the LF signal.
Modeled temperature difference in the battery module from the front (Fr) and back (Bk) while subject to the LF signal.
Modeled temperatures (M) and experimental result temperatures (T) in the battery module and soil while subject to the PV signal.
Modeled battery module temperatures for different thermal conductivities while subject to the LF signal.
Modeled battery module temperatures for different soil specific heat capacity while subject to the LF signal.
Modeled battery temperatures at different ground temperatures while subject to the LF signal.
Model geometry for a square battery pack.
Modeled battery module temperatures and soil temperatures of the hexagonal (Hex) pack and square (Sqr) pack while subject to the LF signal.

Specific heat capacity of thermal pad
Specific heat capacity of steel enclosure plate

RMS error between modeled and experimental results (°C) for both signals.

head.ce:title: Thermal modeling of a subterranean battery energy storage system for residential and commercial buildings

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Renewable Energy Storage Laboratory, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Dalhousie University, PO Box 15000, Halifax, Nova Scotia B3H 4R2, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Renewable Energy Storage Laboratory, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Dalhousie University. PO Box 15000, Halifax, Nova Scotia, B3H 4R2, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novonix Battery Technology Solutions, Bedford, Nova Scotia B4B 1H1, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Novonix Battery Technology Solutions, Bedford, Nova Scotia, B4B 1H1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Battery systems are now routinely deployed at residential and commercial buildings to manage solar photovoltaic generation and electricity demand peaks. The siting of the battery is an important consideration for safety, thermal management, and size (footprint; physical presence). Subterranean (underground) installations have yet to be considered in detail, although they offer benefits in terms of these three considerations. We develop a subterranean battery finite element analysis model to investigate the thermal behaviour and energy performance. The model is calibrated and verified by comparison to experimental testing. The model is then used to investigate performance of the battery while subject to a range of use case application signals, soil thermal conditions, geographic region (soil temperature), and battery geometry. The model can be used to identify the impact of climatic conditions, shape choices, and restrictions on cycling operations due to temperature limitations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion is the foremost battery energy storage technology deployed in residential and commercial building settings due to its high energy density, high power capabilities, high energy efficiency, and long lifetime. A wide range of these units are present in the market and scale in size and shape according to the desired use case and application signal. In residential and commercial building applications, the installation of batteries is “behind the meter” and requires physical space such as a basement, garage or an external structure. Such siting requires footprint and is impacted by the local climatic conditions such as temperature and direct solar radiation. Subterranean battery installation is contemplated as a new method for battery installations, where the system leaves no apparent footprint, thermal management is achieved by dissipating heat to the ground, and safety is enhanced by inaccessibility and non-flammable surroundings.

The performance and lifetime of a lithium-ion battery is strongly influenced by its operating and standby temperatures. Power capability and energy efficiency are impacted at low temperatures (< 5 °C) [1], and the degradation rate increases at high temperatures (> 45 °C) [2,3], leading to a recommended operating temperature range for lithium-ion batteries, often specified by battery manufacturer in the data sheet, from 5 to 45 °C. Additionally, battery temperature is to be kept as uniform as possible during operation, such that one cell or cell group does not degrade differently than the others. Battery thermal management (BTM) and modeling has been studied extensively [4,5], however, all literature focuses on battery installations above-ground.
BTM strategies fall into three principal categories; active, passive or hybrid.

Active BTM systems typically involve the use of liquid or air for cooling or heating, and require additional energy to operate the system regulating the battery temperature. Forced convection is the dominant mode of heat transfer in battery units. The impact of active BTM on the battery thermal behaviour was exhibited in [6], where a 12 Ah cylindrical lithium-ion battery was thermally evaluated with no BTM as well as with active air based BTM. A significant difference in the maximum battery temperature under constant current discharge of 10 A was observed. Where no BTM is used, the battery temperature peaked at 47 °C, which was reduced to 32 °C in the case of active BTM. Yu et al. in their investigation of air based battery thermal management systems [7], report that thermal management is enhanced if the flow of air in the battery is in two dimensions (through the sides as well as the height of the battery). This helps to distribute the air more efficiently and maintain a more uniform temperature within the battery. They also note that thermal control is enhanced with a higher air flow rate providing more effective cooling and keeping the maximum battery temperature at 36 °C, as opposed to 42 °C, where there is no thermal management. Karimi et al. [8] evaluated the thermal performance of water and silicone oil for active liquid BTM, comparing the performance to that of air BTM. In this study, the flow configurations are investigated as well as the effects on temperature distribution for lithium-ion pouch cells. The flow channels are looped around each battery cell, and the inlet and outlet locations are varied for comparison. The findings in this paper show that although the U-configuration (i.e., inlet and outlet locations on one side of the battery) and Z-configuration (i.e., inlet and outlet locations on opposing sides and heights of the battery) are more common designs for liquid BTM, a three-point inlet, and single outlet position provides the best thermal results, ensuring more even temperature distribution within the pack and maintain the battery temperatures below 40 °C. The results also showed that for high discharge-rates (12 minutes discharge or less), air cooling was insufficient to keep battery temperatures below 40 °C, unlike the silicone oil and water. The use of silicone oil consumed higher parasitic power, compared to water, making water the best fluid for thermal management in this study.

Active BTM systems discussed in literature are often studied and optimized based on the flow channel design and arrangement, rate of fluid flow, and the type and flow property of the fluid used for thermal management.

Passive BTM involves the use of the ambient environment alone and no active control mechanisms to regulate the battery temperature. Passive thermal management is a simple approach, depending on heat transfer through natural convection, conduction, or radiation. The battery thermal performance is determined by the natural flow of air, fins, heat plates or pipes or phase change materials (PCM), and the thermal properties of the components within the battery. The investigation of heat pipes as a method for thermal management was reported in [9]. The study was done on a module containing sixteen prismatic lithium-ion cells, and heat pipes were arranged to form a mat. The ‘heat mat’ was positioned under the battery module, absorbing the heat from the battery, and transferring it to an external cooling medium. This method was able to control the battery temperature difference to within 1 °C and maintained the battery temperatures below 30 °C for battery operations under representative duty cycles conditions for electric vehicles. The use of heat pipes or plates alone in literature were found to be insufficient for thermal management in many applications, and thus are often coupled with air or liquid cooling for improved thermal performance. A summary of literature that investigate the performance of heat pipes in BTM systems can be found in [5]. The use of passive thermal management for high-current cycling and high ambient temperatures was explored in [10] using PCM. The PCM used possessed a melting point of 42–45 °C and exhibited sufficient heat absorption capabilities, keeping the peak battery temperature below 45 °C and the temperature difference within 4 °C at a discharge rate of 29 minutes. Whereas in the absence of PCM, a larger temperature difference of approximately 10 °C within the battery was observed. The long-term effect of PCM-BTM was evaluated for one-hour constant current discharge on the battery. The temperature control by the PCM resulted in lower operating temperatures, which reduced the capacity degradation rate to approximately half that of the battery operated with no PCM. Compared to other systems, PCM-BTM provides compactness and reduces the weight of the overall system. However, pure PCM may possess low thermal conductivity and are more expensive than inorganic PCM [11]. Some battery designs may integrate the battery cells directly in contact with PCM, which may lead to displacement in the pack when the PCM melts, resulting in safety issues [12]. Researchers have explored the addition of other materials to PCM for improved battery thermal performance in references [13–15].

BTM systems adopting a combination of active management and passive management strategies may be used in larger battery systems for reasons including cost, simplicity, thermal performance, etc. Hybrid BTM is common for battery applications to maximize battery thermal control. BTM systems may be a combination of active and passive BTM systems, such as heat pipes with liquid flowing through for better heat exchange, or two passive BTM systems such as heat plates and PCM together, or two active BTM systems such as liquid cooling within the battery and forced convection in the ambient environment [5]. Zhao et al. in [16] did an experimental study on the coupled effect of PCM and heat pipes on thermal performance for power batteries containing cylindrical cells. The report shows that PCM only systems accomplished a temperature difference reduction of 33.6 % compared to air BTM systems, however integrating with the heat plate further reduced the difference by another 28.9 % resulting in a temperature difference within the battery of <5 °C. The heat pipe provided support by absorbing heat from the battery and storing it before release to the PCM. This ensures more time for heat absorption and phase transition in the PCM, which has significant effect in cooling the battery and keeping the battery peak temperature below 50 °C.

Thermal modeling of lithium-ion batteries provides a description of the thermal behaviour of batteries during their operation [17]. This includes the heat generation within the battery and the heat dissipation out of the battery into its external environment. Many studies have been done on battery thermal modeling for both design and performance evaluation purposes. The development of thermal models in existing literature often takes into consideration the design features, as well as the operation variables and conditions. The thermal behaviour of lithium-ion batteries may be described with simplified spatial and/or temporal one-dimensional models, two-dimensional models, or complex three-dimensional models [17,18]; they may also be developed as a lumped model. Often times, thermal models are coupled with electrochemical models or equivalent circuit models defining the thermal behaviours as a function of the electrochemical processes in the battery or the operational characteristics of an electric circuit [19].

Chen et al. in [20] developed a three-dimensional thermal model to investigate the heat generation and dissipation under constant current and dynamic power output conditions. The model was useful for describing the temperature profile and temperature distribution within the battery, providing insight for battery thermal management strategies. This work has been used to evaluate different arrangements of cooling and heating channels, and the choice of heating, cooling or insulation materials used in battery design. The work in [21], develops an electrochemical-thermal model for studying the impact of discharge rate, and the ratio of negative to positive electrode capacity, mainly attributed to the thickness of the electrodes, on the heat generation of a battery. A coupled thermal-electrochemical battery model is produced in [22], to describe and predict both the thermal response of a battery and the electrochemical behaviours by combining the thermal energy equation with other electrochemical models based on the temperature dependent properties of the battery. The model provides a tool for predicting the cell electrochemical and thermal behaviours for different charge modes, and to identify mechanisms responsible for thermal runaway. Simple battery thermal models often do not consider the electrochemical reactions, visualizing the battery as a solid homogenous body having average thermal properties with a heat source. These types of models require fewer computational parameters and less processing time, making it easy to implement and understand. They are beneficial where comprehensive electrochemical understanding is not required, thus providing a means for visualizing the temperature distribution of the system.

For subterranean battery operations, BTM can be a challenge as the system is buried underground, necessitating passive thermal management and transfer of heat to the surrounding soil. We present a simplified subterranean battery model to study the impacts on battery performance due to the thermal properties of the soil, the average annual soil temperature, the shape of the battery, and the battery use case application signal. A numerical finite element analysis modeling approach is used. The model results are compared to experimental results to calibrate and validate the accuracy of the model. The development of this model is useful for exploring battery performance for different conditions and applications with varying use cases and applications signals.

The preceding review identifies common BTM methods and the importance of modeling those in determining both new engineering designs and performance evaluation. However, we have not found any studies focused on subterranean batteries. Necessarily, such batteries will rely on a hybrid or passive BTM that rejects heat to the surrounding soil. This presents a unique thermal case that might limit the peak power or continuous duty capabilities of the battery if the heat is unable to transfer to or through the surrounding soil. To address this, we create a new subterranean battery model that is calibrated and validated with experimental measurements and use it to parametrically explore the impacts of signal power intensity, soil temperature and heat capacity, and battery geometry.

A three-dimensional bulk model is developed based on the geometry and major component parts of the system. Thus, the battery pack is treated as a uniform heat generating device with thermal characteristics determined for lithium-ion batteries. Individual cells, cell groups, interconnects and cell support structures are not considered in detail because there are such a wide variety in use today. For example: different cell formats such as cylindrical, prismatic, and pouch; and different positive electrode materials such as nickel‑manganese‑cobalt (NMC) and lithium iron phosphate (LFP). The model is made up of component parts: battery module, mat, steel enclosure, and soil surrounding the battery. The parts are designed and assembled in Solidworks computer aided drafting software and imported to ANSYS finite element analysis software.

The battery pack is designed as a hexagonal unit composed of six flat modules, each having a contact face with the soil, as shown in Fig. 1 . By symmetry of the hexagonal shape an adiabatic condition exists between each sixth, so only a single module must be modeled, along with a “pie slice” of soil. It is assumed in this model that insulation is placed beneath and above the battery, prior to backfilling, and this causes an adiabatic condition on the top and bottom. Thus, all heat generated by the battery module must transfer radially outward through the steel enclosure and through the soil, toward deep ground isothermal temperature, as shown in Fig. 2 . It is unlikely that a true adiabatic condition will exist at the top of any such battery in the field due to the need to input and extract power from the battery, necessitating electrical connections.

An example battery module is assumed to contain cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells of the 21-70 format (i.e., 21 mm diameter, 70 mm axial height). Individual cells are slotted in a cell holder made from modified Polyamide 6, reinforced with glass fibre, mineral and halogen free flame retardant. A thin thermally conductive pad covers the back of the cell holder and is sandwiched between the module and a steel enclosure holding the module in place, with soil behind, as shown in Fig. 3 . The battery cells in each module are connected in the electrical configuration of 22S24P, i.e., 22 cell groups connected in series, each group containing 24 parallel connected cells. Each module is rated nominal 80 VDC, 120 Ah, and 10 kWh.

The battery module is modeled to be a single homogenous entity composed of the individual cells, and cell holder. It is therefore represented as a rectangular prism, of dimensions 0.38 m wide, 1.02 m tall, and 0.084 m thick, for a volume of 0.0324 m3 and mass of 39 kg. The thermal pad behind the module is designed as a thin piece of rectangular material of dimensions; 0.38 m wide, 1.02 m tall, and 0.003 m thick, for a volume of 0.0012 m3 and mass of 1 kg. The steel enclosure plate holding the module is designed as a thin rectangular plate of dimensions; 0.55 m wide, 1.5 m tall, and 0.005 m thick, for a volume of 0.004 m3 and mass of 30 kg. The soil is represented as a trapezoidal prism of height 1.5 m, and a thickness of 1 m representing the distance between contact with the enclosure and the isothermal ground temperature, for a volume of 1.4 m3 and mass of 2500 kg.

The arrangement of the cells in the battery module, gives it a honeycomb-like structure and the thermal conductivity of the module is much greater than that of the soil, hence the temperatures in the module are more uniform. Therefore, the battery module is treated as a uniform internal heat generation element. The cells are positioned horizontally, such that the heat flow from the cell is more efficient along the axial direction than the radial direction, therefore, heat flows through the cell to the enclosure plate and into the soil in the direction along the thickness of module. The steel enclosure of uniform homogenous properties, holds the battery module in place and due to its larger area, provides heat spreading to access more soil area for transporting the heat from the battery module to the soil. The soil is assumed to be homogenous material with uniform thermal properties. This is a logical assumption as backfill soil is compacted during installation of the unit.

The model parameters are defined using input and output variables that define conditions of the system operating in the ground and uses different load profiles involving charge and discharge operations at varying power values. The values chosen for the parameters of the model are representative values of the thermal properties of the component parts in the model and are obtained through measurement or documentation. The model input parameters are listed below, and values for each are provided in Table 1 .
•
Thermal conductivities of component materials, λ
•
Specific heat capacity of the materials, C
p
•
Density of the materials, ρ
•
Rate of internal heat generation in the battery,
q ⃛
•
Heat flux,
q ̇
•
Isothermal soil boundary temperature, T
iso
•
Simulation time, t

The thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, and density of the steel enclosure plate and thermal pad are obtained from published values in material specification sheets. The thermal conductivity and density of the soil are obtained from laboratory tests and measurements, and the specific heat capacity of the soil is estimated. The density of the battery module is calculated using the mass and volume of the battery module. As a function of the battery module modeled as a single entity, averaging the thermal properties of the component parts of the module is adopted to determine the thermal properties: thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity. Thus, the average thermal conductivity,
λ avg
and specific heat capacity,
C
p , avg
of the battery is calculated using the equations,
(1)
λ avg
=
∑ i
ϕ i
·
λ i
(2)
MC
p , avg
=
∑ i
m i
.
C
p , i
where ɸ
i, is the volume fraction of individual components, I in the module,

λ
i, is the corresponding thermal conductivity of component I,

m
i, is the mass of individual components, I in the module,

C
p,I, is the specific heat capacity of component I,
M, is the mass of the module.

The time step of the simulation is determined by the smallest step time of the battery signal to ensure accurate results, while reducing the processing time. In a transient thermal analysis, large time steps (i.e., minutes or hours) may be necessary depending on the analysis, however events occurring under short periods of time must be captured too. For the study in this paper, the battery is cycled with a 24 h long application signal, therefore, a 60 s time step provides good resolution and high accuracy of the results.

The heat rate (watts) generated in the battery during operation is the primary forcing function of the system. The heat rate is influenced by internal resistance (impacted by cell temperature), state of charge (SOC), and cycling power [23,24]. Eq. (3) is a common method, formulated using Bernardi’s model in [25], to determine the heat rate in a battery.
(3)
q = I
V ocv
− V
− IT
∂
V ocv
∂ T
This equation has two terms, reversible and irreversible heat sources in the battery. The first term on the right-hand side describes the irreversible heating in the battery, using three inputs: current, I, terminal voltage, V, and the open-circuit voltage, V
ocv . The second term on the right-hand side accounts for reversible heat as a function of operating temperature, T, and the entropy coefficient,
∂
V ocv
∂ T
. The coefficient changes magnitude and sign as a function of SOC, and consequently the reversible heat can be exo or endothermic depending on the SOC and if the battery is charging or discharging. An overpotential resistance, R, may be defined by dividing the difference in the battery open-circuit voltage and the terminal voltage, by the current, i.e.,
V ocv
− V
I
. Therefore, the irreversible heat term may be re-written as I
2
R, as in Eq. (4).
(4)
q =
I 2
R − IT
∂
V ocv
∂ T

At low SOC (below 20 %), the entropic coefficient magnitude is high, increasing reversible heating impact. However, above 20 % SOC, the coefficient decreases substantially, and its impact further reduces as the cycling rate is increased and the irreversible heating dominates [23,24]. The internal resistance of the battery is relatively constant between 10 and 90 % SOC for a given temperature and cycling rate, the internal resistance of the battery is lower and is not varying notably with SOC [26,27]. As it is typical for stationary battery energy storage systems to cycle between 20 % and 90 % SOC for longevity purposes, the change in the heat rate is substantially influenced by the battery temperature, primarily due to changes in internal resistance that affect irreversible heating, with higher temperatures causing less heat and lower temperatures more [24].

Based on the preceding discussion of battery heating revealing that the heat rate is influenced by SOC and the power signal, the battery operating signals are designed to mimic residential or commercial building applications, requiring an energy throughput that can be satisfied by cycling the battery within a bounded SOC. Two battery signals are adopted for this study. The first is a high-power signal, cycled at varied high rates. The second is a low-power signal cycling at a constant low rate. Each one is designed to cycle the battery within an range of 20 and 80 % SOC. At low power the entropy signal becomes more important relative to the resistive term. However, the reversible term sums to zero over a full cycle, so the long duration temperature changes that take place are not impacted by this signal. Thus, we neglect it as an initial assumption for the purposes of this model and paper, and use only the irreversible term. The drastic changes in the battery internal resistance is also avoided by operating within the SOC envelope, and the internal resistance is assumed a constant value derived from experimental tests. By cycling in this way, the effects of battery degradation, which are accelerated when near fully discharged or charged, can also be curtailed.

The average heat generation rate during a cycle is measured as the difference in the charge and discharge energy and dividing that by the time it takes to complete a given signal, Eq. (5). The division of the average heat rate by the average of the squared currents throughout the cycle provides an effective battery resistance, Eq. (6).
(5)
q ¯
=
CE − DE
t
(6)
R =
q ¯
I 2
¯

CE denotes charge energy, DE denotes discharge energy, t is the signal time to complete the cycle,
q ¯
denotes the average heat rate,
I 2
¯
is the average of the squared currents throughout the cycle and R is the effective battery resistance. While this resistance may not be accurate at any moment in time, it will on average represent the battery which is useful for long term predictions.

The timestep varying heat rate, q
t while the battery is in operation uses the instantaneous current and effective resistance to determine the approximate heat rate in a time step, Eq. (7). By dividing the varying heat rate, by the volume of the battery module, V
b in Eq. (8), the rate of internal heat generation,
q ⃛
t
which is a volumetric quantity, is obtained and is a time-varying input parameter to the model.
(7)
q t
=
I t
2
R
(8)
q ⃛
t
=
q t
V b

The numerical modeling method employs finite element analysis for solving the heat transfer problem in this study through transient thermal simulation. The battery module is represented as a rectangular block, with uniform internal generation. The geometry represents a module built according to industry standards, containing cylindrical cells of 0.07 m in height, and stacked into a block of material designed to hold individual cells and keep the cells spaced apart. The dimensions of the module are given as width of 0.38 m, height of 1.02 m, and a thickness of 0.09 m. The steel back plate is designed as a thin rectangular plate with dimensions of height, 1.5 m, width 0.55 m and a thickness of 0.005 m. The soil medium is represented using a trapezoidal prism, of height, 1.5 m. The parts were created and assembled in Solidworks, and the geometry was imported into Ansys. The input parameters including thermal properties (i.e., thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, and density) are defined in the ANSYS software.

In developing the model, medium hexahedral mesh elements are chosen over tetrahedral due to the simplicity of the geometry and absence of curvature in the structure. This ensures reasonable contact between elements and accuracy with less computation time than tetrahedral methods. This meshing technique results in a total of 11,567 elements and 53,998 nodes. The mesh quality produced an average skewness of 0.2 with a maximum skewness of 0.8, which is below the 0.89 limit of accuracy for hexahedral mesh element.

The initial conditions of the model, where the battery is not in operation and heat is not being generated are uniform due to thermal acclimation of the unit to the isothermal soil temperature. The model assumes that the module, representing one side of a hexagonal battery pack, by symmetry has adiabatic side interfaces with neighbouring modules. Earth heating and surface climatic influence is also ignored due to insulation. Therefore, the boundary conditions for the model are defined by adiabatic conditions on the faces of the model structure, excluding the soil face furthest from the battery. This directs the heat flow from the battery through the soil in one direction (along the radius of an arbitrary outer circle around the hexagon pack). The soil furthest from the battery is considered an infinite medium and thus the ground temperature is unchanging. This is represented by a constant temperature set at this face in the model, as shown in Fig. 4 .

The residential and commercial building signals used for this study are targeted toward a load following (LF) use case and a photovoltaic (PV) solar self consumption use case. The signals are chosen to illustrate common use cases that differ substantially in average and peak power, charge and discharge period length, and dynamic behaviour. Table 2 summarizes test results from a previous experimental study [28].

The experimental study was done to support and validate modeling endeavours of subterranean battery systems operating grid application signals by providing a means of calibration, and parameter identification for developed models.

The round-trip energy efficiency is obtained by dividing the discharge energy by the charge energy over the signal period. The cycle equivalent is defined by dividing the discharge Ah over a signal period by the rated battery capacity in Ah. The signals are described in this section and the profile of the current, voltage, and power are shown for each signal, where the negative current and power values represent discharge mode, and the positive values represent charge mode. The varying heat rates are also shown.

The LF signal is a high-power electrical signal varying over a 24-h period, with a peak discharge current of 98 A (1.2-h rate), and a peak charge current of 49 A (2.5-h rate). The discharge Ah throughput provided by the signal is 327 Ah, which is equivalent to 2.7 full cycle equivalents in 24-h. The signal begins and ends at 80 % SOC. The current, voltage, and electrical power of the signal is shown in Fig. 5 . The varying heat generation rate is calculated based on Table 3 and Eqs. (5)–(7), and is also presented in Fig. 5. It is proportional to the square of the current and always positive.

The PV solar self consumption signal is developed such that the battery stores solar energy produced in the midday where PV generation is at its peak, and the energy is discharged in the evenings when PV is no longer generating and load is high. The signal is represented by a constant current charge over four midday hours, followed by a rest period for 4 h, and a constant current discharge for 4 h during 18-22 h. The signal delivers a discharge amp-hour throughput of 68 Ah, equivalent to 0.57 full cycles equivalent in a 24-h period. The signal is a light duty signal cycled at a 6.5-h rate (18.5 A) and maintains an SOC band of 20 % to 80 %. The current, voltage, power profile and the varying heat rate generated from the signal is shown in Fig. 6 .

Soil temperatures are denoted by “yzz” referring to temperatures measured from different locations in the soil, where “y” is the radial location in the soil and “zz” is the position at that radial. The battery module peak temperature is labelled “M_Mod_Max”, and soil temperatures results from the model are labelled, “M_Soil_yzz” where “y” is the radial location in the soil and “zz” is the position along the radial and height, according to those defined in Fig. 7 , and are compared to measured experimental results denoted as “T_Mod_Max” and “T_Soil_yzz”. The heat conduction is analyzed using the soil temperatures and the battery peak temperature. The center point of the module face is estimated to be the point where heat conduction is the least efficient due to limitations on the heat pathways from that point, therefore the battery temperature is most likely to be the highest around the center. Whereas the top of the battery module has additional access to soil to transfer heat, as shown in Fig. 7b. Readers can compare the model results to experimental results in the subsequent figures.

Experimental results were obtained from a yearlong research project [28]. The experiment was composed of a battery module formed by 21-70 cylindrical nickel‑cobalt‑aluminum lithium-ion cells. The cells were arranged in a 22S24P format, rated 80 VDC, with coulombic capacity of 120 Ah, and energy capacity of 9.6 kWh. The cells are placed in a thermally conductive polyamide 6 cell holder and mounted to a stainless steel enclosure plate by a thin layer of thermally conductive pad. The plate is interfaced with the soil having density 1906 kgm-3, thermal conductivity of 2.8 Wm-1 K-1. T-type thermocouple sensors are placed on the battery module and throughout the soil as per the locations shown in Fig. 7. A battery cycler was used to charge and discharge the battery module in accordance with the profiles given in Section 2.5. Experimental results were recorded at a 1 s timestep. Readers are encouraged to see [28] for additional information on the experimental unit, including photographs and a complete listing of test equipment.
The heat power, maximum battery temperature and soil temperature results are shown on time-series plots and are discussed in this section.

Fig. 8 shows the 24 h application signal, preceded by a 2 h charge to achieve the 80 % SOC starting point; this is shown as it affects the initial temperature of the module and soil. The model predicts the battery temperature rising quickly as heat is generated during the application signal, and increases, peaking at 47 °C, as the signal progresses. The soil at position 1 nearest to the battery responds immediately to rising battery temperature, as heat conduction occurs. The heat continues to flow through the soil prompting the soil temperature at position 2, 0.375 m away from the battery, to also increase, albeit slowly and rising only by 3 °C according to the model predictions. At 0.75 m away from the battery, the soil temperatures appear to not have any significant temperature rise from the heat transported through the soil during the first 24 h. Although temperature decreases occur as the heat rate decreases, it is not sufficient to return the battery temperatures back to initial values, or even below 35 °C. Therefore, the LF application signal cannot be operated continuously, day over day, without overheating the battery. This signal is too aggressive for continuous duty in these subterranean conditions. However, if the soil temperature at the isothermal boundary were 15 °C, as could be the case in Northern climates, then the battery would not overheat based on the results shown here.

The battery temperatures according to the model results shows smoothness, whereas the test result shows some unique behaviour and noise. This unique behaviour may be attributed in part to the contribution of reversible heating, which may be endothermic or exothermic within the bounded SOC of the battery during the operation of this signal. This is something not presently captured in the model. Additionally, the test result gives lower overall temperature values, and this can be attributed to unintended heat losses through the insulation of the experimental test; whereas the model assume adiabatic conditions.

Fig. 9 shows the temperature difference between the hottest front face (Fr) of the battery module and the coolest back face (Bk) of the battery. Through the period of the battery operation under the LF signal, the battery temperatures remain within a tight boundary, below 5 °C temperature difference. The temperature difference attains a maximum of 4.1 °C at the point of peak power output in the signal. This is below the recommended limit of 5 °C maximum difference in temperatures within the battery [29,30], and peak power occurs infrequently in the signal and lasts for short durations of 5 minutes. Due to high thermal conductivity of the battery as a function of the cell orientation in the module, the heat is conducted throughout the battery quickly and the average temperature difference in the battery through operation of the LF application signal is 1 °C. This shows good uniformity of the temperatures in the battery.

The solar signal is a low power signal with lengthy rest periods where cooling occurs, in the 24-h period. Operations are shown throughout the daytime, followed by an extended overnight rest period. As shown in Fig. 10 , the model predicts the battery temperature rising promptly as cycling begins, however the temperature only reaches to 33 °C due to the low power of the signal. The heat from the battery transferring through the soil causes an immediate response of the soil at position 1, nearest to the battery. During the rest period the battery reduces in temperature. The soil temperature further away at position 2 appear is only marginally impacted, increasing by <1 degree Celsius through the cycle. At position 3, the soil temperature appears unaffected. The extended rest period in between daily cycles is sufficient for conduction of this heat quantity and brings the battery temperature back to its initial temperature.

The measured temperature peaks of the battery and soil at position 1 during 4–5 h into the cycle are greater than the modeled values. This is unexpected because the model assumes adiabatic conditions on all but one side of the battery. It illustrates that for this low power application signal, the impacts of uniformity imposed by the model, such as soil packing and contact resistances, give a better cooling performance than can be obtained in practice.

In the period between 10 and 12 h into the cycle, the test results show a decrease in the battery temperature, whereas the predicted battery temperature continues to increase. This may be attributed to a more notable contribution of reversible heating, which is endothermic on discharge between 40 and 60 % SOC [23]. In this model, the reversible heat is ignored in the determination of the internal heat rate, as this is dominant within at low SOC, which is outside the SOC range of the battery signal. However, the results of the low power signal show that reversible heat consideration is necessary in determining the heat generation rate for increased accuracy of the battery temperature response to low power applications. Importantly however, the subterranean battery can continuously operate this application signal day after day without overheating. And it trends to the ground temperature, which may be used in determination of degradation impacts.

The validation metric used in this work is the root mean square error (RMSE) between the model result temperature values (MV) and the test result temperature values (TV). This is given by Eq. (9).
(9)
RMSE =
∑ i
TV − MV
2
n i
where n
i is the number of entries. The root mean square error between model and experimental results is summarized in Table 3 .

Low RMSE values are desired for high accuracy of model results. Maximum RMSE between the model results and experimental results is 1.75 °C, indicating good agreement and reliability of the temperatures nearest to the battery (position 1), and that of the LF signal with significant heat rate. This error can be attributed to unintended heat loss through the insulation of the test unit during experimentation, estimation of the soil heat capacity, discrepancy in measuring the soil thermal conductivity during testing, etc.

A sensitivity and parametric analysis of the model input parameters was carried out to study the influence on the temperature distribution and draw conclusions relating to the heat conduction rate and battery temperature for different scenarios such as changing soil types (e.g. sand, clay, etc.), ground conditions, climatic factors (cold or warm climates), and battery shape. Heat conduction is evaluated in this model, hence the related thermal properties; thermal conductivity and heat capacity are varied. The consistent deep ground temperature is also varied, to evaluate underground battery operation in different climate locations. The sensitivity of these variables on the model output provides information on the impact of the inaccuracy of the parameter on the model accuracy, and for parameter identification purposes in subterranean battery modeling. The sensitivity and parametric analysis are done using the single variable method, where variation of the parameters is done independently, and keeping the other input parameters constant. This investigation is completed using the LF signal due to the notable variation in the temperatures observed from the result.

The thermal conductivity of the soil varies due to porosity in the soil, the moisture content as well as the solid mineral composition in the soil [31,32]. To analyze the sensitivity of the thermal conductivity, three different values of thermal conductivity; 1, 2, and 4 Wm-1 K-1, are input to the model simulating battery operation of the LF signal, and the difference in model temperatures is evaluated, as shown in Fig. 11 .

Fig. 11 shows the battery temperature is sensitive to the thermal conductivity of the soil. The variation of this parameter does not change the shape of the temperature trend. Rather the effect is seen in the rate at which the temperatures rise (heats up) and fall (cools down) through cycling of this signal. The measured battery temperature is most aligned with the model results where the soil thermal conductivity, λ is 2 Wm-1 K-1. Doubling the thermal conductivity might be expected to have a greater temperature reduction, but this is not the case due to other series thermal resistances (e.g. pad) and the heat capacity of the battery itself. For battery operations, high thermal conductivity is preferred to reduce both the average and peak battery temperature. This may be achieved by different backfill types, increased compaction, and consistent moisture in the soil.

The heat capacity of the soil can be varied by packing factors, moisture, and the material composition of the soil [32,33]. To analyze the sensitivity of the specific heat capacity, three different values of specific heat capacity; 500, 1000, and 2000 Jkg-1 K-1, are input to the model simulating battery operation of the LF signal, and the difference in model temperatures is evaluated, as shown in Fig. 12 .

Fig. 12 presents the battery temperature for varied values of the soil heat capacity. The soil heat capacity does not change the shape of the battery temperature curve and has a lesser impact on magnitude than changes to soil thermal conductivity. The measured battery temperature is most aligned with the model results where the soil heat capacity is 1000 Jkg-1 K-1. Doubling the heat capacity of the soil does lead to reduction in the battery temperature, however, this impact is reduced due to the long timeframe dynamics and the thermal conductivity of the soil. Obviously, it is desirable to increase the soil heat capacity to limit average and peak temperatures of the battery, but it could also be advantageous in cold climates to maintain the battery temperature when it is in standby. The degree to which soil heat capacity can be increased is limited due to material selection.

The deep ground temperatures around the battery module will impact the battery operating temperature. This is important for two reasons. First, the average temperature of the battery during operation and the time it spends at high temperatures contribute to its degradation. Second, where the battery power output is high, significant heat rate will increase the battery temperatures and may result in overheating in the battery. Ground temperatures of 20 °C, 25 °C, and 30 °C are applied to the model and the model results are analyzed, as shown in Fig. 13 .

The impact of increasing or decreasing the deep ground temperature is shown in Fig. 13 and bias offsets the battery temperature as soil temperature is increased. This is an important consideration for high-power signals with considerable heat generation rate such as the LF application. The battery operation of these types of signals continuously is impeded by the hotter deep ground conditions as the likelihood of overheating is higher and the rate of degradation increases. For low power signals, which generate less heat, the risk of overheating is reduced and the battery will trend toward deep ground temperature on average. In especially hot or cold climates, the battery may be buried further underground to mitigate these extremes.

A different shape of the battery pack is used to explore the difference in the thermal performance. The model developed and studied is originally hexagonal shaped. However, it may be more practical to have a square shaped battery pack, hence, a battery pack with four identical battery modules, one on each side, is investigated. As seen in Fig. 14 , each side holds an identical sized battery module as the hexagonal pack, but now possess a wider area and volume of soil for the heat generated from each module to be transported through. This means that for each module, there are more heat pathways for conduction of heat, and increased volume of soil for improved heat storage. Of course this comes at a reduced overall battery energy capacity and overall power, which are both two-thirds of the original hexagonal pack. This is of less use per unit footprint area.

Fig. 15 show the battery temperature results for a square battery pack, compared to that of a hexagonal battery pack. The heat conduction rate is higher for a square battery pack compared to the hexagonal pack. This is revealed in the slope of the temperature decline, and results in lower soil temperatures and consequently lower battery temperature in the square pack. Therefore the square pack cools faster than the hexagonal pack. This is due to the quantity of heat pathways in the soil, and the volume of soil available to each module to contain the heat generated from the battery.

Seeing as the square pack cools faster, it appears that the square-shaped pack has a thermal advantage over the hexagonal pack. While there is a difference in the output temperatures, the maximum difference observed between the battery temperatures is 2 °C. This is not a large variation in the results, suggesting a lower sensitivity of the battery geometry in the model output compared to the other parameters such as the soil thermal properties. Of course, the square battery pack contains only four modules in series, which is two-third of the capacity of the hexagonal pack containing six module. For energy intensive applications, this thermal advantage may not be prioritized, but for power-intensive applications, the square-shaped design may appear to be the more optimal geometric design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion and recommendation for future research
ce:section.ce:para:
A subterranean battery thermal model was developed to study the behaviour and performance in operation for residential and commercial building applications. The model results were validated against experimental results, from a 1/6th scale system measured over a one-year period, to assess accuracy. We conclude that:
•
Subterranean battery operations for low-power signals, with less than one full cycle equivalent per day, and high energy efficiency (>95 %) can be done while keeping the battery temperature within the nominal temperature range (<40 °C). For continuous operation of low-power signals such as the solar PV, the battery temperature returns to its initial value of 30 °C after each charge (midday, 10:00-1200) and discharge (evening, 18:00-22:00) cycle. However, subterranean battery operation of high-power signals over many days may lead to significant temperature increases well beyond 45 °C, triggering upper temperature limit shutdown. This will increase the rate of battery degradation and may cause failure. For example, the LF signal has 2.7 cycle equivalents per day, which is high average power, and the battery temperature remains elevated at the end of a cycle, inhibiting further consecutive cycles of the same type.
•
The thermal properties of the soil have a significant impact on the average and peak battery temperature, with thermal conductivity making up to a 10 °C difference, and specific heat capacity a 5 °C difference. The backfill soil can be chosen to prefer heat capacity for systems located in cold weather environments. The backfill soil thermal conductivity or compaction can be increased in hot weather environments, while recognizing the battery will trend to the deep ground temperature or hotter on average.
•
The deep ground temperatures influence battery temperatures with a bias offset. However, the deep ground temperature has no impact on the heat conduction rate in the soil to speed up cooling or heating, so increased ground temperatures reduce the tolerable peak and average operating power of the battery. This is especially pertinent given that the battery can be buried in a wide range of ground temperatures, and it is certain that subterranean batteries experience similar cycling will degrade faster in hotter ground temperature environments.
•
Changing the battery geometric structure has an influence by accessing additional ground surface interface areas, and additional thermal mass of the ground. This could lead to unique designs. However, the heat from the battery primarily travels in a straight line rather than propagating to the sides, so while reduced module count (e.g. 3, triangle) will reduce temperatures by (5 °C or less), the loss in energy capacity will be substantially more impactful and the battery less useful. The nature of the application is an important consideration in choosing the geometry of the battery dependent on what is most necessary – energy capacity or power capabilities.

Future research should focus on refining the model for a more granular cell level study, improving the accuracy, and providing more information on the temperature distribution in the battery module itself. Other possible studies from this work, may be targeted at investigating the influence of a gradient in the thermal conductivity and ground temperature along the length of the battery modules on the battery thermal performance. The model may also be adapted to include heat flow through the top as a function of surface climatic conditions as well as the bottom and studying the resulting temperatures. The mechanism of identifying the heating rate in this model ignores reversible heat consideration. However, it is evident that for low power signals, it is important to take this into account. Therefore, the model may be improved to include reversible heat reactions in the battery. This will be more apparent in the short-term cycle results and will likely have no effect on the long-term battery temperature progression.

Elizabeth Oyekola: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lukas Swan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. J.R. Dahn: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was made possible with funding from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, #NETGP 468468-14) through support of the Strategic Network of Energy Storage Technology (NESTnet) based at Toronto Metropolitan University (formerly Ryerson University). Additional support was provided by the Vanier-Banting Secretariat, the Province of Nova Scotia through Graduate Scholarships, and the Faculty of Engineering, Dalhousie University. The support and provision from the NOVONIX group, Canada and the Renewable Energy Storage Laboratory, Dalhousie University is appreciated. We also appreciate residential and commercial building data obtained from EMERA Energy.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new perspective on soil thermal properties
Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. E. Ochsner, R. Horton, and T. Ren, “A New Perspective on Soil Thermal Properties,” Soil Science Society of America Journal, vol. 65, no. 6, pp. 1641–1647, 2001, doi: 10.2136/sssaj2001.1641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific heat capacity of soil solids: influences of clay content, organic matter, and tightly bound water
Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, Y. Lu, R. Horton, and T. Ren, “Specific Heat Capacity of Soil Solids: Influences of Clay Content, Organic Matter, and Tightly Bound Water,” Soil Science Society of America Journal, vol. 83, no. 4, pp. 1062–1066, 2019, doi: 10.2136/sssaj2018.11.0434.
